message.content:
You are an adversarial production-readiness assessor for the hc-assay engine at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (a dataset-agnostic, data-sovereign empirical-ML/methodology engine; optional backends import-guarded; transcripts committed + scrubbed intentionally). It has survived EIGHT hardening passes and CI is currently green — easy findings are gone. Report ONLY genuine, concrete, FALSIFIABLE production-readiness defects with a real failure mode and a file:line/symbol location; no style nits. If a dimension is clean, return few/zero findings — do NOT invent. Read real code (Read/Grep/Bash; "python -m pytest", "source .venv/bin/activate"). The branch is harden/pass-9 (just branched from merged main @ pass 8).

YOUR DIMENSION: privacy/PII.
Re-probe scripts/capture_transcripts.py + the committed corpus for any remaining operator-identity vector or new secret/PII class. The separator-flexible handle regex, IGNORECASE name tokens, ReDoS, and zero-pattern-derivation contexts were covered in pass 7 — look for anything NEW (other identities, hostnames, IPs, tokens, the .uv-exclude-newer or new files leaking anything).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll investigate the privacy/PII dimension for the hc-assay engine, focusing on new operator-identity vectors and secret/PII classes not covered in pass 7.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
90	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
91	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
92	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
93	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
94	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
95	        if v:
96	            names.append(v)
97	    try:
98	        import subprocess
99	
100	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
101	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
102	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
103	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
104	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
105	        pass
106	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
107	    seen: set[str] = set()
108	    for n in names:
109	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
110	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
111	        seen.add(n)
112	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
113	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
114	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
115	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
116	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
117	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
118	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
119	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
120	        toks = n.split()
121	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
122	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
123	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
124	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
125	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
126	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
127	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
128	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
129	        for t in toks:
130	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
131	                seen.add(t.lower())
132	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
133	    return pats
134	
135	
136	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
137	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
138	
139	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
140	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
141	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
142	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
143	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
144	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
145	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
146	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
147	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
148	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
149	    """
150	    handles: list[str] = []
151	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
152	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
153	    try:
154	        import subprocess
155	
156	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
157	        if out.returncode == 0:
158	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
159	                handles.append(m.group(1))
160	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
161	        pass
162	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
163	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
164	    expanded: list[str] = []
165	    for h in handles:
166	        expanded.append(h)
167	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
168	            expanded.append(h[3:])
169	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
170	    seen: set[str] = set()
171	    for h in expanded:
172	        key = h.lower()
173	        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
174	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
175	        seen.add(key)
176	        if len(h) >= 8:
177	            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
178	            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
179	            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
180	            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
181	            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
182	            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
183	            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
184	            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
185	            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
186	        else:
187	            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
188	    return pats
189	
190	
191	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
192	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
193	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
194	
195	
196	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
197	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
198	        if pat.groups == 2:
199	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
200	        elif pat.groups == 1:
201	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
202	        else:
203	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
204	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
205	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
206	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
207	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
208	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
209	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
210	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
211	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
212	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
213	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
214	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
215	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
216	    return text
217	
218	
219	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
220	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
221	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
222	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
223	
224	
225	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
226	    try:
227	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
228	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
229	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
230	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
231	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
232	        #
233	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
234	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
235	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
236	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
237	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
238	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
239	        # present) is copied verbatim.
240	        if b"\x00" in raw:
241	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
242	        else:
243	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
244	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
245	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
246	        else:
247	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
248	        return {
249	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
250	            "bytes": len(data),
251	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
252	        }
253	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
254	        return None
255	
256	
257	def main() -> int:
258	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
259	    if not src.is_dir():
260	        return 0
261	    session = _active_session(src)
262	    if session is None:
263	        return 0
264	    sid = session.stem
265	    captured: list[dict] = []
266	
267	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
268	    if rec:
269	        captured.append(rec)
270	
271	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
272	    subtree = src / sid
273	    if subtree.is_dir():
274	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
275	            if f.is_file():
276	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
277	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
278	                if rec:
279	                    captured.append(rec)
280	
281	    try:
282	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
283	        MANIFEST.write_text(
284	            json.dumps(
285	                {
286	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
287	                    "active_session": sid,
288	                    "n_files": len(captured),
289	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
290	                    "files": captured,
291	                },
292	                indent=2,
293	            )
294	            + "\n",
295	            encoding="utf-8",
296	        )
297	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
298	        return 0
299	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
300	    return 0
301	
302	
303	if __name__ == "__main__":
304	    sys.exit(main())
305

content.text: Now let me probe the committed corpus for leaks the current scrubbing misses. Let me check for IPs, hostnames, and other identity vectors.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && ls -la transcripts/ && echo "---" && du -sh transcripts/sessions 2>/dev/null && echo "---FILES---" && find transcripts -type f | head -50

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && cat .uv-exclude-newer 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; ls -la .uv-exclude-newer 2>/dev/null; echo "--- git config ---"; git config user.name; git config user.email; echo "--- remote ---"; git remote -v; echo "--- home ---"; whoami; echo $HOME

content.text:
Confirmed: the scrubber passes the hostname through unmodified. This is the primary finding.

Now let me check for any OTHER new identity/secret class. Let me look at the `transcripts/README.md` to see what's documented as in-scope, and probe for: macOS UUIDs, serial numbers, the project absolute paths in subprocess output, Anthropic account/org IDs, and session UUIDs that could correlate.

content.content:
1	# Transcripts (provenance)
2	
3	This directory captures the **Claude session transcripts** behind the work in this repository,
4	so the provenance and effort of the agent interactions can be reviewed and audited alongside the
5	code.
6	
7	## What's here
8	
9	- `sessions/<session-id>.jsonl` — the main session transcript (one JSON object per line: prompts,
10	  tool calls, tool results, model output).
11	- `sessions/<session-id>/subagents/*.jsonl` — transcripts of subagents spawned in that session.
12	- `sessions/<session-id>/workflows/...` — workflow scripts and their subagent transcripts.
13	- `MANIFEST.json` — what was captured, when, and a SHA-256 of each file.
14	
15	## How it's captured
16	
17	A committed git hook (`.githooks/pre-commit`, enabled via `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`)
18	runs `scripts/capture_transcripts.py` before every commit, which snapshots the **active** session
19	(the most-recently-modified transcript) and its subtree into `sessions/`, then stages it. So
20	**every commit pulls in an up-to-date transcript snapshot.**
21	
22	To enable the hook on a fresh clone: `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`.
23	
24	## Scope & caveats (read before publishing)
25	
26	- Credential-shaped secrets (API keys, OAuth/bearer tokens) are **redacted** on capture.
27	- Transcripts still contain **local filesystem paths** and may contain the **operator's email**
28	  and internal reasoning. They are legitimate provenance, but if this repository is made
29	  **public**, review this directory first — consider scrubbing further or excluding it
30	  (`git rm -r --cached transcripts/` + a `.gitignore` entry).
31	- These files are **not** shipped in the built wheel (only `src/assay_engine` is packaged).
32	- Transcripts are append-mostly JSONL, so they delta-compress well in git history.
33

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== Anthropic org/account IDs ==="; grep -rohI -E "(account|org)[-_]?id[\"']?\s*[:=]\s*[\"']?[a-f0-9-]{16,}" transcripts/ | sort -u | head; echo "=== ssh-rsa / private keys ==="; grep -rlI -E "BEGIN (RSA|OPENSSH|EC|PGP) PRIVATE|ssh-rsa AAAA" transcripts/ | head; echo "=== macOS UUID/serial ==="; grep -rohI -E "Serial Number|IOPlatformUUID|[A-Z0-9]{10,12}\b.*serial" transcripts/ | head; echo "=== gh auth token api ==="; grep -rohI -E "ghp_[A-Za-z0-9]{10,}" transcripts/ | grep -v REDACTED | head

content.content:
<persisted-output>
Output too large (201.9KB). Full output saved to: /Users/[REDACTED]/.claude/projects/-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/tool-results/blcaaq3iq.txt

Preview (first 2KB):
=== Anthropic org/account IDs ===
=== ssh-rsa / private keys ===
=== macOS UUID/serial ===
REGRESSION from a prior hardening fix
35	
36	**Location:** `src/nodes/gates.py:246`
37	
38	### Problem
39	When hard prerequisites fail, gate_2_node returns early (gates.py:238-261) writing failure_events: [{event_type: 'gate_prerequisite_failure', ...}] plus a deferred gate_status — and it does this WITHOUT going through _run_gate, so the _already_decided() guard (gates.py:135) is never consulted. failure_events has an append reducer (state.py:65: lambda a,b: a+b). 'deferred' is intentionally non-terminal, so on any re-entry of gate_2 while prereqs are still unmet the early-return runs again and appends another identical gate_prerequisite_failure event. The Gate-2 reject loop (route_after_gate_2 rejected → exploratory_phase → … → gate_2) and operator re-triggers both re-enter gate_2; each pass that still fails a prereq duplicates the failure event. Agent Evolution monitors failure_events for routing re-optimization (state.py:64, orchestrator.py:16) and will see the same prerequisite failure counted N times, biasing the signal and bloating the audit record.
40	
41	### Best practice violated
42	Nodes that loop or re-execute (here via the Gate-2 reject→exploratory→…→gate_2 loop and via operator re-trigger after a deferred park) must be idempotent with respect to append-reducer state. A side effect placed before/around interrupt() and not guarded by a terminal check will re-fire on every re-entry.
43	
44	### Fix
45	Route the prerequisite-failure path through the same terminal/idempotency discipline as decisions: before emitting, check whether gate_status[gate_2] already records an identical deferred prerequisite-failure for the current unmet set and skip re-appending the failure_event (return only the gate_status update, or {}). Alternatively, dedupe failure_events with a reducer that drops duplicates by (event_type, gate_id, failures), mirroring the eda_arti
...
</persisted-output>

content.text:
No org IDs, no private keys, no serial numbers. The README explicitly documents email/paths as in-scope-but-known but does NOT mention hostname. This strengthens the finding: hostname is an *undocumented, unscrubbed* operator-identity vector.

Let me do one final targeted check: the README says "operator's email" may be present but the email IS redacted now — minor doc drift. And let me confirm the hostname leak is the genuinely new vector by checking it appears in actual tool_result content (subprocess output) vs only assistant prose, and verify `.local` mDNS names are operator-identifying.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && f="transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/subagents/workflows/wf_82001839-351/agent-a61cd08ca3c6d93a2.jsonl"; echo "=== is hc-grc.local in tool_result (real output)? ==="; python3 -c "
import json
for line in open('$f'):
    if 'hc-grc.local' in line:
        try:
            o=json.loads(line)
        except: continue
        t=o.get('type')
        msg=o.get('message',{})
        role=msg.get('role')
        print('type=',t,'role=',role)
        c=msg.get('content')
        s=json.dumps(c)
        i=s.find('hc-grc.local')
        print('  ...',s[max(0,i-80):i+20])
"
input.description: Determine if hostname is in real subprocess tool output

content.content:
=== is hc-grc.local in tool_result (real output)? ===
type= user role= user
  ... \n95\t@prefix prov: <http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#> .\n96\t@prefix hcgrc: <https://hc-grc.local/prov#>

content.text:
This is a genuine, non-meta leak. `hc-grc.local` appears in a `user`-role message as actual file content — a PROV-O RDF prefix `hcgrc: <https://hc-grc.local/prov#>` that was read from a real file in the hc-grc project. This is not a subagent discussing the hostname; it's the operator's internal hostname embedded as a namespace URI in committed provenance data. The scrubber misses it entirely.

I have a solid, falsifiable primary finding plus a minor doc-drift finding. Let me record them.

findings.title: Operator's internal hostname ([REDACTED] / hc-grc.local) leaks into committed transcripts; no scrub pattern covers it
findings.location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-216 (_SECRET_PATTERNS + _scrub: no hostname rule); leaked in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/subagents/workflows/wf_82001839-351/agent-a61cd08ca3c6d93a2.jsonl and wf_496c86b1-0fc/agent-a6c39cf0b12e80f58.jsonl and wf_574c42b7-0aa/agent-ad2e7e5365e7451dd.jsonl
findings.claim: The capture scrubber redacts email, home-dir username, display name, and GitHub handle, but has NO rule for the machine hostname. The operator's internal hostname appears in the committed corpus as the strings '[REDACTED]' and 'hc-grc.local' (14 raw occurrences across 2+ files). At least one is genuine data, not meta-discussion: a `user`-role message embeds the PROV-O RDF namespace `hcgrc: <https://hc-grc.local/prov#>` read from a real hc-grc project file. `hostname`/`scutil --get LocalHostName`/`--get ComputerName` on this node all return [REDACTED], and per the operator memory this Mac mini is the named production node, so the hostname is operator/infrastructure-identifying PII.
findings.why_real: Verified live: `scutil --get LocalHostName` and `ComputerName` both return `[REDACTED]`; `hostname` returns `[REDACTED]`. Importing the scrubber and calling capture_transcripts._scrub('ran on [REDACTED] node') returns the string UNCHANGED — confirming no pattern matches the hostname. The string is present in committed files (grep -rl finds it), so it ships in the repo and git history. This is a distinct identity class from everything pass 7 covered (handle/name/username/email/ReDoS).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hostname-derivation rule analogous to _username_pattern/_repo_handle_patterns: derive candidates from socket.gethostname(), platform.node(), and (on macOS) `scutil --get LocalHostName`/`ComputerName`, plus an ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS env; for each (length-guarded, >=4 chars) redact the bare name and the `<name>.local` mDNS form case-insensitively. Run it inside _scrub after the existing rules. Add a regression test asserting [REDACTED] is redacted, and document the hostname class in transcripts/README.md scope/caveats.

findings.title: transcripts/README.md scope caveat understates redaction (email) and omits the new identity classes actually scrubbed/leaking
findings.claim: The README says only 'Credential-shaped secrets ... are redacted' and that transcripts 'may contain the operator's email and internal reasoning.' In reality the scrubber now redacts email/username/display-name/GitHub-handle (so the email claim is stale — emails ARE redacted to [REDACTED]@protonmail.com), while the hostname ([REDACTED]) is NOT redacted and is NOT mentioned. A reader relying on this caveat to decide whether to publish gets an inaccurate picture of both what is removed and what residual identity vector remains.
findings.why_real: Grep confirms emails in the corpus appear only as `[REDACTED]@protonmail.com` (local part scrubbed), contradicting 'may contain the operator's email'; meanwhile `[REDACTED]` appears raw and is unmentioned. The doc is the operator's stated gate for public release (line 24 'read before publishing'), so its inaccuracy is a real pre-publication-decision hazard, not a style nit.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the caveat to: (a) state emails, home-dir username, operator display name, and GitHub handle are redacted; (b) list the residual classes that are NOT scrubbed (filesystem path structure, internal reasoning, and — until P9-PII-1 is fixed — internal hostnames); keep the email line accurate (domain remnant only).

content.content: Structured output provided successfully

content.text:
I completed the privacy/PII re-probe of the pass-9 branch. Summary of what I found and verified:

**Primary finding (medium) — undocumented, unscrubbed hostname identity vector.** The scrubber in `scripts/capture_transcripts.py` covers email, home-dir username, display name, and GitHub handle (all pass-7 territory), but has no rule for the machine hostname. On this node `scutil --get LocalHostName`/`ComputerName` return `[REDACTED]` and `hostname` returns `[REDACTED]` — the named production node per operator memory. The strings `[REDACTED]` and `hc-grc.local` appear raw in committed transcripts (14 occurrences). At least one is genuine data, not a prior auditor's meta-discussion: a `user`-role message embeds the PROV-O namespace `hcgrc: <https://hc-grc.local/prov#>` read from a real hc-grc file. I imported the scrubber and confirmed `_scrub('ran on [REDACTED] node')` returns the string unchanged.

**Secondary finding (low) — stale README scope caveat.** `transcripts/README.md:26-30` (the operator's stated pre-publish gate) says only credential-shaped secrets are redacted and emails "may" be present, but emails are in fact redacted to `[REDACTED]@protonmail.com`, while the unscrubbed hostname is not mentioned.

**Things I checked and found clean** (no findings): public IPs in the corpus are all test/example/well-known addresses (8.8.8.8, 1.2.3.4, 93.184.216.34, 169.254.169.254) discussed in code/audits, not operator-identifying; no Anthropic org/account IDs; no SSH/PGP private keys or `ssh-rsa` material; no macOS serial/IOPlatformUUID; `ghp_`/`sk-ant-` tokens only appear as `[REDACTED]`; the email local-part is uniformly redacted (only the `@protonmail.com` domain remnant survives, which is documented intent); `MANIFEST.json` contains relative paths only; `.uv-exclude-newer` holds only a date (`2026-06-14T00:00:00Z`), no identity; global git config is empty and `[REDACTED]` is the hostname, not a committer name.

Relevant files: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/README.md`, and the leaking transcripts under `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/subagents/workflows/`.
